Amen.<|7.90|> <|9.90|> It is the second Sunday after Pentecost and the first Sunday of June, LGBT Pride Month.<|21.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This year marks the 50th anniversary of Denver's first<|6.40|> <|6.40|> pride observance, 1974.<|12.44|> <|12.44|> By 1974, Denver's LGBTQ+ residents had used the previous year<|20.12|> <|20.12|> to campaign for codified protections in the law for their<|26.70|> <|26.70|> sexual orientation.<|29.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In 1973, activists flooded a Denver City Council meeting<|7.00|> <|7.00|> to demand an end to law enforcement's harassment<|11.00|> <|11.00|> of gays and lesbians.<|14.00|> <|14.00|> The overwhelming demonstration at that council meeting<|18.00|> <|18.00|> resulted in, quote, "The council repealing four laws<|23.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> that the Denver Vice Squad used to arrest and harass LGBT people,<|8.00|> <|8.00|> sometimes ruining their lives and reputations.<|13.00|> <|13.00|> And less than a year later, Denver Pride was founded.<|18.00|> <|18.00|> So this is now 1974.<|21.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> According to one succinct observer, before there was pride, there was revolt.<|10.00|> <|10.00|> Before there was pride, there was revolt.<|16.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Pride commemorates the series of times LGBTQIA+ people have asserted our humanity in the face of discrimination.<|12.00|> <|12.00|> The first Pride wasn't even called Pride. This is 1970 in New York City.<|20.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But rather, it was called Christopher Street Liberation Day, referring to the location of the Stonewall Inn, the site of an uprising of LGBTQ plus people against New York City police in a gay bar raid.<|20.40|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> before pride, a revolt, both in New York City and in our own Denver.<|11.74|> <|12.74|> This history must not be forgotten because it is unfortunately our present.<|19.48|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> There are currently 515 anti-LGBTQIA bills under consideration in state legislatures across the country.<|13.00|> <|15.00|> 515.<|17.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> "In our own state, citizens are seeking to codify same-sex marriage in the Colorado Constitution<|9.10|> <|9.10|> to protect same-sex couples in the event that the Supreme Court of the United States overturns<|17.12|> <|17.12|> equal marriage federally."<|20.76|> <|20.76|> And this is a little civics lesson.<|22.68|> <|22.68|> You don't want the preacher giving a civics lesson.<|25.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Our state constitution defines marriage as a union of one man and one woman, but this is negated by the Supreme Court's 2015 ruling.<|16.30|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Coretta Scott King's words sting us in their wisdom.<|5.00|> <|5.60|> Struggle is a never ending process.<|8.60|> <|10.60|> Freedom is never really won.<|13.04|> <|13.04|> You earn it and win it in every generation.<|16.04|> <|18.68|> It cannot be taken for granted.<|21.38|> <|21.38|> It cannot be done.<|22.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> forgotten.
